speaker: interviewer
text: You went from Kaluszyn to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Warsaw to register that I am alive--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and see if there's any other people from Kaluszyn--

speaker: interviewee
text: --or from ar-- other areas that I know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I did know that the people that lived in Kaluszyn, the people that had the factory of the sheepskins, went to Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to Lodz, and we settled in Lodz, uh, for a [INAUDIBLE]. We worked with them. And then we got ourself an apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Me and my brother, and my brother married.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got ourself an apartment, and we lived there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, the first few months in 1945, the people from the-- from the sheepskin factory and I went out to look-- to do something. Because people were traveling into Germany and back, and doing business, and buying this, and selling that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you move freely back and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we could-- we could move.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not freely. You had to be lucky--

speaker: interviewee
text: --not to be caught. So my friend and I--

speaker: interviewer
text: In other words, you were-- you were really, uh, crossing the border illegally.

speaker: interviewee
text: Crossing the border-- the border crossing wasn't that bad, as, uh, occasionally there were some patrols on the road--

speaker: interviewee
text: --that were more dangerous than the-- than the crossing the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish patrols, or Polish-- or Polish, uh, secret service.

speaker: interviewee
text: They want to know who runs away, who's-- because there were many, many Poles that used to run away, uh, from-- from Poland to Germany because they did not behave.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, they were working with the Germans. And some of them used to come back, and they were wanted. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we went there. We got money. Where did I get it? But-- yeah, the friends of mine--

speaker: interviewee
text: --the one-- the from the sheepskin. They had work. And they had money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you sold the house, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you have enough money to live on, or did you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any agency supporting you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. At that time, it was no-- it was-- maybe there was agency. But we didn't go to an agency because we-- my brother and I sold our house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had no problem. We got the money right away. The only thing what we didn't do right is we didn't know what to do with the Polish money, which are the war dollars. And we had the Polish money that-- and devaluated. But with the money that we had, we did business, and we made all right. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of business did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to buy materials to going in Germany. We bought materials to bring into-- to-- to Warsaw to sell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to buy-- he changed the German Marks. They used to be-- and on the railroad used to be-- the Russian workers-- they used to be brought into Germany to work-- had to leave Germany. And they came with German Marks. And the German Marks, they couldn't do anything. At the-- at the border, we used to buy from them the German Marks and go into Germany, buy with those German Marks better-- other materials. And this is how--

speaker: interviewee
text: Wheel-- like you say, wheeling and dealing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wheeling and dealing. How long did you, um, live in Lodz and wheel and deal in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till almost the-- December 1945, maybe even before.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we decided that, uh, there's nothing that we gonna do, anything in Poland. We will go to Palestine. At that time, it was Palestine. And to go to Palestine, we have to go to Germany, because from there it will be easier to go, whatever we want to do, and we'll have contact.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came to Berlin, the-- the family from the [INAUDIBLE]. Their name was Berman. The family name was Berman. There were three brothers and a brother-in-law, uh, about our ages-- a year or two. Who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Jews from our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were the ones who had-- oh, they were the ones who had the sheepskin.

words.text: They were the ones who had--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were the Bermans. And we stayed together, and we worked together like, uh-- like one family. We went out together. We came to Berlin. And we decided that Berlin, although a-- a lot of Jews settled in Berlin to do all kind of businesses. We decided to go to Munich, to an area in Bayern, in Bavaria.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it-- I don't remember, uh, the reason, because we heard that there is a town called Landshut. And there is a landsman of ours that came from Mauthausen, from a camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they-- the-- a cousin of mine and I remained in Berlin because we had some bus-- unfinished business. And they, my brother and his wife and the rest of the Berman family, left for Landshut, for that place.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no camp, no DP camp, just-- in-- in early-- early 1946, they were still-- the Germans-- they were still-- not to say afraid. But they still had no-- no-- no reason to say, no, we don't want you. We don't give it to you. They were giving it unwillingly, but very quietly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my brother got an apartment. Everybody got apartment, nice apartments. And we lived privately.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there we-- we got the-- the UNRWA, the-- the United Nations rehabilitation.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Joint Distribution Committee used to s-- send us help for food. And we organized a-- a Jewish committee to pray every Saturday-- Fridays and Saturdays. And the later days came, they built a DP camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they built-- they came in more-- more Jews came into Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they had to have them concentrated in one place. They couldn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: --take anymore houses from the Germans, because the Germans also lived close.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And when did-- when-- where was that DP camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: Landsb-- is that the same area?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, not the same area.

speaker: interviewee
text: Landshut is, um, about 65 kilometers east of Munich, towards Regensburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Towards Regensburg-- toward the east side, toward Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. How did you get into Berlin? Did you get--

speaker: interviewee
text: Legal-- we just went. What would they do to us? There was a time that the Germans-- the Germans could not, uh, say to the Jews, hey, you don't belong here, something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- they were glad that we're not touching them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Russians did not care. There were still open borders that you could go in here and go out there. And, uh, it wasn't that open.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you could go through. There were drivers that used to carry you. They knew when to travel, when to get by, and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm, yeah. And, um, did-- did you get to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. When we came-- when we came to Landshut, we-- I-- I, uh, registered to go to Palestine. But before-- but that time was Aliyah Bet. We couldn't go legally. And the-- the British had the w-- the warship. They did not let it go through. And at the same-- while I was still in Berlin, my-- uh, there came in some news from landsmans, from Landsmannschaft in-- in New York. K-- Kalushiner Landsmannschaft, in New York, heard that there are some Kalushiner in Germany. They got in touch with us there. And they will try to help us. The Joint Distribution Committee, or the-- the, uh, UJA. At that time, we used to call it Joint Dist--

speaker: interviewee
text: --registered people that want to go to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was registered. Uh, when I came-- after my brother left for Landshut and I remained in Berlin, they registered to Palestine, and they registered to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: So whichever comes first, I will go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I came in 1946-- sometimes in April-- March or April, I came to there. I was already registered here and there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it came in. About a year we waited for, um, the visas to come from United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got the visas in-- in, uh, sometimes in March.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got the visas to come to the States. And, uh, in April of 1947, we arrived in New York on

speaker: interviewee
text: The ship was a troop-- a troop ship--

speaker: interviewee
text: And, uh, we were taken into Hot-- to Hotel Marseilles on 103rd Street, off Broadway.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there for a few weeks. And I start to-- to look up if I have any relatives. And, uh, we went out to look for an apartment. And I got two apartments in Brooklyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, who brought you across when--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Joint. And, uh, I was looking for apartments. And on the ship, when we came over, I met a friend of mine that we were in camp together in Lagischa. And being that you find a friend from those good old days--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we found two apartments. Well, one apartment for me, and one apartment for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we moved in-- lived on 214 Watkins Street in Brooklyn, if you know where it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I don't know Brooklyn. Um, and what kind of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. What kind of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, then it came, uh-- um, the Joint tried to get me in a-- a job. But-- but the job I was, I had no-- no skills-- to get me to work for a grocery store to deliver groceries. It wasn't to my liking. But I could not refuse it because they, uh, paid us, uh, some money to buy food for the time being. My brother got a job at-- a distant cousin had a-- had a place, a refrigeration place. So, uh, got a job there. And, uh, we-- we managed with even-- without the Joint. We didn't-- I didn't want to take from the Joint any money for food. And separately, we found out that we had relatives in Costa Rica--

speaker: interviewee
text: --which is in Central America. Those relatives used to be importers to Costa Rica, buying merchandise in New York. When they found out that we're here, they came to see us, asked us what we can do to find me a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they worked with-- with a forwarder. Uh, do you know what a forwarder is? A forwarder is a company that arranges the-- the shipping out merchandise from here to foreign countries-- prepare the papers, have arranged with the ship to take the merchandise in, and so on. Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they needed-- they asked him, do you have maybe a job? Here's a young man, came from this-- from Europe. Uh, and there were a few-- a few relatives, two-- one, uh, [INAUDIBLE] from my mother's side. There are a few-- two families that they were their customers, in other words. And they could not refuse him. He says, yes, we'll take him. He'll work as a messenger.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I became a messenger. And I started. And there-- there was a-- maybe, uh, six, eight messengers in the company. It was a big company. And they told-- they gave me papers to go to this steamship company or to that bank, and give it to this one or that one. All I have to do is deliver papers and bring papers back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And slowly, I went to school, to night school. I finished night school very easy, and-- being that on the job, I learned the language pretty fast. And, uh, came a-- a time that I can be proud of that business got slow. And they-- and they let go a lot of-- a lot of messengers. But they didn't let me go-- let me go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because whatever they couldn't do-- the American boys, they couldn't do the job, uh, to arrange whatever they were suppose to. They sent me. Because when I came there not knowing the language, they had to call up the main office, say, what do you want? There is a man that doesn't know what he wants. (LAUGHING) So I was the one.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I learned so fast that I got a job inside making-- on a cal-- on a copyrighting machine-- not like we have today. But there was a machine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, it's called a duplicating machine.

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- and I worked there for, uh-- let's see. 1947 till-- about a year and a half. And I got a raise. I got-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] I started with $35. Then I got $40 a week. And I was happy. The relatives of mine in Costa Rica, in-- but separately, we-- my brother and I started to do business, to work, to peddle. We used to peddle stockings, pantyhose-- not-- not pantyhose. There were no pantyhose-- stockings, blouses, skirts. Used to go on farmers' markets. And the relatives in Costa Rica saw that we're not sleepy. We can do something. I became their agent-- their buyer, their agent here. They needed material, certain textiles. They sent me in a sample, a swatch.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I used to go to the places where they used to buy, or other places, to ask him-- show him this. Do you have this material? How much is it? How much a yard? Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: How many yards of this? All kind of information. And I used to make commission. That I started-- and then we started the business into export business that we used to sell all the items that we needed. We used to travel to Central America, to South America. And we became a company, Kishel Brothers Trading Company.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, after a few years, we incorporated. And we were a company since 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: You'll be-- 1949. In two years, you opened your own company.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Your, um-- let's go back a little bit to the not such nice times.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your greatest fears while you were going through the ghettos, the-- the--

speaker: interviewee
text: My greatest fear-- that I won't be able-- that I won't live to see the end of it. Because, well, we were-- the greatest fears were at the time we were on the march. When we were on the march, there was nothing that you could hide from.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's say you were in a camp. You could hide under a bed. You could hide someplace. On the march, you are on the outside. No, uh-- on the fields-- no-- nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was especially-- one time, we were on the march. There was a wagon. No horse-- we were the horses. A wagon-- and the Germans put all their equipment on the wagon. And we used to-- on the wagon, the-- the one at the front used to pull. And we in the back and the sides used to push.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Germans went on-- on-- on the front and the back. To the guys in the front, they should not pull that fast. And to us, they beat us up not to push that fast. So no matter where you went, where you were on the wagon, you got beaten up. That was the worst place that you could ever happen. The front wasn't good. The side wasn't good. The rear wasn't good.

speaker: interviewee
text: And behind-- behind the wagon we were walking, because not everyone was in the wagon. They used to change. And I was carrying my two friends-- they should not fall down. Because if they'll fall down, they-- they're gonna be shot during the-- and finally, I noticed that on the side road, there was a piece of apple. You know, the-- the core of an apple was laying there in the snow. And I left them standing. And I ran out and catch it. The German noticed me. [GERMAN], you know-- you can run. To the-- to the front to pull. And when I-- and the guy in front of me, you know, he put on like-- like a--

speaker: interviewee
text: A harness on yourself. He took off the harness. And I put it on me and says, all right, what can I do? This is it. Do you know-- [SOBBING] I have to cry. Not a drop of anything fall on me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I always said that even if I got touched-- you know, some people, they got hit. They laid down. They didn't want to do it. The Germans tell them, get up. And they didn't want to. They beat them. They let-- beat them to death. I said, I am not going to do it. They'll beat me. I'll get up, and I won't let myself. I have to see the end. [SOBBING] With this in my mind, I survived. I have to see the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that why you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Get myself support-- that I have to see them s-- lose.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. [SIGHS] When-- uh, what happened when you came-- uh, after you'd gone into this business, when did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: See, my wife is from Costa Rica.

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- I mean, she immigrated to Costa Rica in 1936. Her father immigrated in 1929--

speaker: interviewee
text: --because it was bad times in Poland. And they were there. And being that, uh-- uh, they had a business, she came to buy for their store.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my, uh, relatives, they-- they introduced us. I always knew I want to get married. And she also want to get married. And we liked each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: '48. And, uh, we decided that we'll get married.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to Costa Rica. And we got married in Costa Rica.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came back to the States. And here we are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm, and you have two daughters--

speaker: interviewee
text: And four-- each one has a couple.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. How, um-- how much were your children aware of your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at the time they-- they were small, they knew something would happen. But later on, we were talking about. And they were reading the papers. And there were shows, and they were on televisions. And they know what we went through.

speaker: interviewee
text: And some people were hiding-- not to tell them. And, uh, I was trying to tell them as much as possible. Because you know, children, they are thinking of it-- it's-- it's bad. So I did not push. Occasionally, I mentioned it and, uh, tell a story about this or about that. And they listened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your experiences have any influence on the way you raised your children, your two daughters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Will you please say the question again?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your experiences in the Holocaust, did it have any influence on how you raised your two children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'll tell you. Uh-- the influences in the Holocaust did not experience much. [SOBBING] It's the home that I came from.

speaker: interviewee
text: [MOAN] Showing us how to behave, how to [INAUDIBLE]-- how we were raised.

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- there have been homes that children were raised, always beaten, always this. And we were not beaten. We did not, as some children were-- stealing from parents or things like that. We had enough. We didn't have to do anything. We were loved. And we loved our parents. So this is how we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you, um, involved in, um-- and your children. Are any of them involved in Holocaust activities or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, uh, I belong to American Gathering and [INAUDIBLE]. And my daughter belongs here also. And I think my daughter in St. Louis belongs also. Whenever there comes something, they do a-- they do attend. And they do want to know what happened, and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: In terms of education and, um-- and Jewish life, are they involved--

speaker: interviewee
text: They're very-- very well Jewish-minded. The children go to Hebrew schools.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, matter of fact, uh, my oldest granddaughter--

speaker: interviewee
text: --uh, just got a trip, a free trip to Israel because she attended the-- and she still attends the Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the children, um, in St. Louis know very well, uh, reading, reading the Torah, reading the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you dream much about your-- your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Occasionally. Occasionally. I'm not, uh-- not-- not much. I'm not trying to think during the days. Or sometimes, maybe occasionally, uh, you meet people and-- uh, that we were in the same camp. So we talk about it during the day. And-- but we're not talking-- not like-- like we tell the story how it was here. We-- it's different. It's different. We're not trying to go out and keep-- I was here. You have to have respect for me. I'm not coming up-- advertise. Look, you-- I was here. And you should, uh, keep your mouth shut because, uh, I was there. I'm not trying to-- to promote myself-- that because I was there and I survived that I'm a-- I'm a big shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever gone back since, uh, the war was-- since you're here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I wanted to go back. But I don't wanna go back by myself. I wanna-- I wanna-- At least, I wanna go back with a friend of mine that we were together, or to take my wife along. But my wife is not a traveler. And especially in this respect, you know, she's, uh, very touchy. She doesn't wanna go.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, uh, I hope-- I hope to have, uh-- to go back to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, by then, I guess [INAUDIBLE]. But, uh, I would like to go back to Auschwitz to see how it is there, because I-- I can visualize. I can draw the map of where things were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Have you ever gone back to Kaluszyn or Warsaw after you came back here?

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you just went. Mhm. Is there anything else that you would like to add to your story before we show the pictures and have your children and grandchildren on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, uh, at the moment, I don't, uh, recall anything more to add. Unless something-- uh, you see someone, or something comes, uh, about-- about torching-- about-- about the lamp. And, oh, that lamp. Something has happened with the lamp, and I remember. Nothing that I recall to it. But, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Any advice to the younger generation?

speaker: interviewer
text: Any advice to the younger generation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what advice? The younger generation knows the-- the story of World War II. So what kind of advice you want me to give them? To-- to see that no such regime [SOBBING] ever happens in any place of the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: Especially when today we have, in-- in-- in Yugoslavia and Serbia that it happens. Although, I feel with the Serbs. I feel with the Serbs. And you'll ask me why. Because-- cause the Serbs are the underdogs in Yugoslavia. The Croats and the Germans were like this. And the-- the Bosnians-- I mean, the-- the Muslims, I don't know about them. But the Croats were terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Serbs h-- helped all the Jews. So this is-- uh, those that don't feel, uh, what the-- the Serbs went through. And there are countries or nationalities that are taking revenge-- revengefuls.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Serbs may be vengeful. And they have reason to be vengeful. But it has to be a limit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Has to be a limit. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to, um, not-- in the interview now, we have a fair number of pictures we want to show.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we want to have your, uh, wife and children.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know whether they will be on. So that, uh, we're gonna cut the tape now and start showing the pictures and then have some--
